---
title: "The engine: Viash and Viash Hub"
description: "How components are built, containerized, tested, and published."
order: 40
---

OpenPipeline's [modularity](modularity.qmd) is not hand-maintained, it is
produced by two pieces of tooling: **Viash**, which builds each component, and
**Viash Hub**, the platform that builds, tests, and publishes them.

## Viash: config + script → component

**[Viash](https://viash.io)** turns a config (`config.vsh.yaml`) plus a script
into a self-contained component. From that one description it generates:

- a **command-line interface**, parsing and injecting the declared arguments into
  the script;
- a **Docker image** pinning the component's exact dependencies;
- a **Nextflow (VDSL3) module** that can be chained into a workflow.

Viash separates a component's **logic** (a script in Python, R, or Bash) from its
**execution environment** (the container) and its **orchestration layer**
(Nextflow). Two properties fall out of that separation:

**Language independence.** Each component is written in whatever language suits
the task, normalization in Python, a statistical test in R, a file conversion in
Bash. The generated CLI makes them interchangeable regardless of language.

**Modularity.** Because every component shares the [MuData](mudata.qmd) format
and a generated CLI, they compose freely, no glue code between steps. The same
component runs standalone with `viash run`, is tested with `viash test`, or is
built into an image with `viash build`.

## The technology stack

At build time Viash compiles each component into a Nextflow module. Workflows
chain these modules using Nextflow DSL2, with data flowing as MuData files on a
shared channel. The same pipeline runs unchanged from a laptop to an HPC cluster
or the cloud.

```{mermaid}
%%| label: fig-techstack
%%| fig-cap: "Components (code in R, Python, or Bash) are wrapped by Viash into Docker images and Nextflow modules, assembled into Nextflow workflows that run unchanged on local, cloud, and HPC platforms."
%%{init: {"theme":"base", "themeVariables":{"fontSize":"14px"}}}%%
graph LR
    classDef input fill:#f5f5f5,stroke:#aaaaaa,color:#444
    classDef light fill:#eaf5e6,stroke:#b3d99e,color:#000
    classDef mid   fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#82b366,color:#000
    classDef core  fill:#a9d69a,stroke:#5a9e57,color:#000

    MuData["<b>MuData</b>"]:::input
    R["<b>R scripts</b>"]:::light
    Python["<b>Python scripts</b>"]:::light
    Bash["<b>Bash scripts</b>"]:::light
    Viash["<b>Viash component</b>"]:::core
    Docker["<b>Docker images</b>"]:::mid
    Modules["<b>Nextflow modules</b>"]:::mid
    Nextflow["<b>Nextflow workflows</b>"]:::core
    Local["<b>Local execution</b>"]:::light
    AWS["<b>AWS Batch</b>"]:::light
    GCP["<b>Google Cloud Batch</b>"]:::light
    HPC["<b>HPC</b>"]:::light
    More["<b>...</b>"]:::light

    MuData --> R & Python & Bash
    R & Python & Bash --> Viash
    Viash --> Docker & Modules
    Docker & Modules --> Nextflow
    Nextflow --> Local & AWS & GCP & HPC & More
```

## Viash Hub: build, test, publish

**[Viash Hub](https://www.viash-hub.com)** is the platform that runs this
machinery continuously. It acts as the CI backing OpenPipeline: it **builds the
containers**, **runs the unit and workflow tests**, and **publishes** the
resulting components to a versioned catalog. Because the same configs drive both
the build and the docs, it is also what lets the
[reference](../reference/index.qmd) be auto-generated.

From the catalog you can browse components, read their parameters, and launch
workflows through a GUI, or connect to Seqera, without touching the command
line. Each generated reference page links to its Viash Hub entry for copy-paste
run instructions.
